Idiom (San Nicolaas English): Throw stone and hide your hand
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Coordinated verb phrase with bare imperatives; fixed idiomatic clause using object noun plus possessive noun phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To cause trouble secretly and then pretend to be innocent when people start asking questions.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Start a problem and deny responsibility for it.
Usage Contexts: Daily speech; family disagreement; workplace blame; politics; sarcasm; community gossip.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A widely shared Caribbean English Creole idiom, natural in San Nicolaas talk where indirect conflict, yard news, and public reputation often shape how blame is discussed.
First Example Sentence: I know he throw stone and hide your hand, because he tell the supervisor one story and act quiet in the meeting.
Second Example Sentence: Don’t throw stone and hide your hand by the yard, just say plain what you vex about.
Third Example Sentence: She throw stone and hide your hand when she spread the rumor and then ask who start it.

Idiom (San Nicolaas English): Empty barrel does make most noise
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Proverbial declarative clause; singular noun phrase plus habitual marker “does” and comparative superlative noun phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: People with little knowledge or substance often talk the loudest.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: The least informed person is often the loudest speaker.
Usage Contexts: School; public debate; workplace; family advice; humor; social media criticism.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common across Caribbean English varieties and fitting San Nicolaas public-life humor, where argument, calypso commentary, and street discussion value wit over empty boasting.
First Example Sentence: Leave Calvin alone, empty barrel does make most noise and he ain’t read the report yet.
Second Example Sentence: The boys in the shop laugh, because the empty barrel does make most noise every time football come up.
Third Example Sentence: Auntie say empty barrel does make most noise, so I listen more to the quiet mechanic than the loud one.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptical proverbial clause; plural body-part noun phrase plus habitual auxiliary “does” and stative verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone who refuses good advice will eventually suffer the consequences.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: If you ignore warnings, you will experience the result yourself.
Usage Contexts: Parenting; safety warnings; school discipline; friendship advice; neighborhood caution.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A Caribbean moral proverb preserved in household speech, especially where elders teach younger people through short, memorable warnings.
First Example Sentence: I tell you not to ride fast by the wet corner, but hard ears does feel.
Second Example Sentence: Hard ears does feel, and now the same man who laugh at the rule waiting outside the office.
Third Example Sentence: Granny only shake she head and say hard ears does feel when the cake burn in the oven.

Idiom (San Nicolaas English): When drum call, foot must answer
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Subordinate temporal clause followed by modal main clause; personified nouns in a fixed proverb-like structure.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A strong cultural pull or personal calling is difficult to resist.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: People respond naturally to what deeply moves them.
Usage Contexts: Carnival; music; dance; identity; cultural pride; storytelling; motivational speech.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in San Nicolaas performance culture and wider Caribbean drum, calypso, carnival, and road-march traditions connected to community gathering and public joy.
First Example Sentence: I was tired from work, but when drum call, foot must answer, so I reach the band practice before dark.
Second Example Sentence: When drum call, foot must answer, and even the quiet children start moving by the parade truck.
Third Example Sentence: He say he done with Carnival, but when drum call, foot must answer and he sewing costume again.

Idiom (San Nicolaas English): Don’t put sea salt on fresh cut
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative imperative clause with concrete object phrase and prepositional complement; metaphorical injury frame.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Do not make someone’s pain, embarrassment, or problem worse.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Do not worsen a sensitive situation.
Usage Contexts: Condolence; apology; conflict resolution; friendship; family; workplace sensitivity.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on coastal life and the everyday experience of sea water stinging a cut, a vivid image in San Nicolaas and other Caribbean seaside communities.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, San Nicolaa: /doːn pʊt siː sɔlt pan frɛʃ kʌt/
First Example Sentence: She already shame about the mistake, so don’t put sea salt on fresh cut in front everybody.
Second Example Sentence: When his father pass, the cousin choose quiet help instead of putting sea salt on fresh cut.
Third Example Sentence: Boss, don’t put sea salt on fresh cut; correct the invoice, but don’t mock the new clerk.

Idiom (San Nicolaas English): Cockroach ain’t got business in fowl dance
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative existential-possession clause with animal noun subjects and prepositional setting; fixed cautionary proverb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Do not enter a risky place or conflict where you are clearly vulnerable.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Avoid situations where you are likely to be harmed.
Usage Contexts: Street wisdom; politics; workplace power dynamics; humor; warning; family advice.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A Caribbean animal-proverb pattern adapted naturally to San Nicolaas speech, using comic contrast to warn against careless involvement with stronger people or forces.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, San Nicolaa: /ˈkakroːtʃ eːn gat ˈbiznəs in foːl dans/
First Example Sentence: Leave them managers to quarrel, because cockroach ain’t got business in fowl dance.
Second Example Sentence: He want jump in the big boys’ argument, but cockroach ain’t got business in fowl dance.
Third Example Sentence: I tell my nephew cockroach ain’t got business in fowl dance when he start teasing the guard.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Reduplicated numeral phrase functioning as subject; habitual marker “does” plus causative-result verb phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Small steady efforts can achieve a large result over time.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Consistent small actions eventually produce meaningful progress.
Usage Contexts: Saving money; studying; business planning; fitness; community projects; encouragement.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A local-feeling variant of a wider Caribbean incremental-effort proverb, resonant with households, shops, and workers who value steady saving and patient building.
First Example Sentence: Keep learning five words a day, one-one drop does full bucket.
Second Example Sentence: The youth group collect small donations, and one-one drop does full bucket by festival time.
Third Example Sentence: I ain’t rich, but one-one drop does full bucket, so I save little from every paycheck.

Idiom (San Nicolaas English): Don’t measure Baby Beach with refinery ruler
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative imperative clause with place-name object and metaphorical instrument phrase; locally anchored comparative idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Do not judge one person, place, or situation by the wrong standard.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Evaluate things according to their own purpose and context.
Usage Contexts: Cultural criticism; education; urban planning; tourism; business; art review; identity debates.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Contrasts Baby Beach’s coastal leisure image with San Nicolaas refinery history, capturing the town’s blend of beach life, industry, work, memory, and changing identity.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, San Nicolaa: /doːn ˈmɛʒə ˈbeːbi biːtʃ wid rəˈfainri ˈruːlə/
First Example Sentence: The visitor complain the town ain’t like the hotel strip, but don’t measure Baby Beach with refinery ruler.
Second Example Sentence: Don’t measure Baby Beach with refinery ruler; the mural walk and the machine yard telling different stories.
Third Example Sentence: Teacher say don’t measure Baby Beach with refinery ruler when we compare village speech to textbook English.

Idiom (San Nicolaas English): Mouth does run before mind lace up
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Personified body-part subject with habitual marker “does”; temporal subordinate phrase with metaphorical phrasal verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone speaks too quickly before thinking carefully.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Speak less impulsively and think first.
Usage Contexts: Apology; arguments; classroom; customer service; leadership; social media; teasing.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects Caribbean verbal play and San Nicolaas conversational wit, turning quick speech into a comic race where thought has not yet put on its shoes.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, San Nicolaa: /maut dɔz rʌn bifɔː main leːs ʌp/
First Example Sentence: I had to apologize, because my mouth does run before mind lace up when I vex.
Second Example Sentence: His mouth does run before mind lace up, so the aunties make him repeat the story slower.
Third Example Sentence: Before you post that comment, remember mouth does run before mind lace up.

Idiom (San Nicolaas English): Old zinc know how rain does sing
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Metaphorical declarative clause; adjective-noun subject plus cognition verb and embedded interrogative content clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Experienced people understand trouble before inexperienced people recognize it.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Experienced people can recognize signs of difficulty early.
Usage Contexts: Elder wisdom; weather talk; politics; family planning; workplace risk; reflective storytelling.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Evokes Caribbean zinc roofs and household memory; in San Nicolaas, the sound of rain on old roofing becomes an image for elders who have lived through storms, refinery shifts, and social change.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, San Nicolaa: /oːl zɪŋk noː hau reːn dɔz sɪŋ/
First Example Sentence: Ask Miss Lydia before you sign, because old zinc know how rain does sing.
Second Example Sentence: The young crew laugh at the warning, but old zinc know how rain does sing when money start missing.
Third Example Sentence: Old zinc know how rain does sing, and Grandpa hear trouble in that plan from the first meeting.
